title: practical english usage - Part 312
content:
Note also: an electric shock; an electric atmosphere (full of excitement).

an electric motor electric blankets Electric is used with the names of particular machines that work by electricity.

446electric        and   electrical two; an eldest son/daughter is the oldest of two or more.

olden eldest is used when there are more. An elder son/daughter is the older of Elder brother/sister are used when a person lias only one brother/sister who  is She's the oldest student in her year.

- His eldest/oldest daughter is a medical student.

He's three years older than me. (     ... elder than me/) - My elder/older brother has just got married.

(before nouns). Compare: lhe order of birth of the members of a family. They  are only used attributively Elder and eldest can be used instead of older and oldest to talk about 445 elder      and   eldest Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  448  end and finish: verbs ► the    British     Isles     411 England,       Britain,     the    United       Kingdom        and not I finished teaching at 3.00. (     I ended teaching. ..) Finish, but not end, can be followed by an -ing form (►   100).

Nouns that end in -s have plurals in -es.

The road ended in a building site. (     The road finished...) End is often used to talk about physical shapes.

The ceremony ended with a speech from the President.

How do you end a letter to somebody you don't know?

or 'shaping' the end of something.

We   also prefer end to talk about a special way of bringing something to a close The Second World War ended in 1945.

It's time to end the uncertainty - the Prime Minister must speak out.

I decided it was time to end our affair.

End is more common when there is an important change.

‘ Are you still reading the paper? ‘ No, I've finished.’ You'll never finish that hamburger - it's too big for you.

She's always starting something new, but she never finishes anything.

prefer finish.

When we  talk about completing something that we  are doing, we  usually 2 completing        an   activity Term ends/finishes on June 23.

What time does the concert end/finish?

These verbs have similar meanings, and are often both possible.

448 end     and   finish:     verbs If you are not satisfied with my hospitality, go elsewhere.

This is a formal word for somewhere else.

You'd better stop hitting my little brother, or else!

Or else is sometimes used with no continuation, as a threat.

Let's go, or else we'll miss the train.

Or else means 'otherwise', 'if not'.

I didn't see any other people. (     .. .any else people.) There is no plural structure with else.

4 singular      only vocabulary7 •  450  enough There was just enough light for us to see what we were doing.        -> It's late enough for the staff to stop work.

Infinitives can be introduced by for + noun/pronoun.

I haven't got enough money to buy a car.

She's old enough to do what she wants.

We   can use an infinitive structure after enough.

4 enough        + infinitive;      structure       with    for (= We   need bigger nails - enough modifies big.) We haven't got big enough nails.

(= We   need more big nails - enough modifies big nails.) We haven't got enough big nails.

When  enough modifies an adjective and noun together, it comes before the 3 position      with    adjective       + noun I don't understand enough of the words in the notice.

I don't know enough Spanish to read this, (     ... eneugh-of Spanish . ..) enough of. Compare: Before another determiner (article, possessive, etc) or a pronoun, we  use If only we had time enough ...  I was fool enough to believe him.

except in a few expressions.

Enough is occasionally used after a noun, but this is rare in modern English Have you got enough milk? There aren't enough glasses.

Enough can be used before a noun as a determiner (►   171).

2  enough         + noun You're not driving fast enough.

Is it warm enough for you? (     ... enough warm . ..) Enough usually follows adjectives and adverbs.

1  adjective/adverb           + enough 450 enough not I don't enjoy looking after small children, (     .. . enjoy to leek ...) Enjoy can be followed by -ing.
